sections.header: Verses 1-6 — The Tav Mark and the Six Executioners

heb.paragraph: The man in linen marks the foreheads of the faithful with a tav — the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet. In the ancient paleo-Hebrew script, tav was written as a cross or X-mark. Church fathers saw this as a foreshadowing of the cross of Christ marking believers for salvation amid judgment.

The man clothed in linen (baddim) wears priestly garments — linen is the fabric of holiness (Lev 16:4). This figure acts as both priest and scribe, marking the righteous for protection. He contrasts with the six executioners who follow him.

refs.note: The Passover blood on doorframes protected Israelites from the destroying angel — the tav mark echoes this protective sign.
Angels seal the servants of God on their foreheads before judgment falls — directly echoing Ezekiel's vision.
Judgment begins at the house of God — Peter's principle matches Ezekiel 9:6, where the slaughter starts at the sanctuary.

notes.note: Six men with destroying weapons come from the direction of the upper gate — the north, the direction of Babylon. The agents of judgment approach from the direction of the coming army.
The mark on those who grieve shows that God distinguishes between the faithful remnant and the apostate majority. Not all in Jerusalem share the guilt.
Begin at my sanctuary — judgment starts where privilege was greatest. The elders before the temple are killed first because they bore the greatest responsibility.

Calvin teaches that the mark identifies those who share God's own grief over sin. True faith is not passive but actively mourns the dishonor done to God. Indifference to surrounding sin is itself a form of complicity.
Calvin emphasises 'begin at my sanctuary': God's judgment is most severe where his name has been most dishonored. Greater proximity to grace means greater accountability.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb 'groan' (ne'enah) and 'lament' (ne'enaq) describe visceral, audible grief — not mere internal disapproval but vocal protest against the abominations.
The 'upper gate facing north' is the same gate where the image of jealousy stood (8:3-5) — the location of greatest offense becomes the entry point for judgment.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block connects the tav mark to covenant theology: just as circumcision marked the covenant community, the tav marks the faithful remnant within the condemned city. The sign creates a new covenant community defined not by ethnicity or geography but by moral and spiritual response to sin.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli sees the seven figures as the divine council executing judgment — a heavenly court scene enacted on earth. The man in linen serves as the mediating figure between the divine throne (ch. 1) and the human city, combining priestly and scribal functions.

hist.context: God summons six agents of judgment: one scribe in linen with a writing kit, and five executioners with destroying weapons.

sections.header: Verses 7-11 — Will You Destroy the Entire Remnant?

heb.paragraph: Ezekiel cries out: 'Will you destroy the entire remnant (she'erit) of Israel?' This is one of only two times Ezekiel intercedes (cf. 11:13). Unlike Abraham bargaining for Sodom or Moses interceding for Israel, Ezekiel's intercession receives no concession — God's response is absolute. The sin of Judah and Israel is 'exceedingly great.'

refs.note: Abraham interceded for Sodom and found less than ten righteous — Ezekiel faces a similar moment but receives a harsher answer.
God told Jeremiah: 'Do not pray for this people' — both prophets learn that intercession has limits when judgment is decreed.

notes.note: Ezekiel's cry — 'Will you destroy the entire remnant?' — shows the prophet's pastoral heart beneath the harsh message. He cares about the people even as he announces their doom.
God's justification: the land is full of bloodshed, the city full of injustice. They said 'The LORD has forsaken the land' — the same theology of 8:12 that justified idolatry.

Calvin reads Ezekiel's intercession as a model for all ministers: even when God's judgment is certain, the faithful servant cries out on behalf of the people. The answer may be no, but the prayer is still required. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'the land is full of blood' (male'ah ha'arets damim) echoes Genesis 6:13 where violence filled the earth before the flood — the parallel implies total judgment. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block notes that Ezekiel's intercession is modeled on Moses (Num 14:13-19) but receives Moses' worst-case answer. The Mosaic pattern of sin-intercession-pardon has reached its breaking point — the covenant mechanism of forgiveness has been overwhelmed by the weight of accumulated sin.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli highlights the theological irony: the people claimed 'YHWH has forsaken the land' (8:12, 9:9) to justify idolatry, but it is their idolatry that will cause YHWH to forsake the land. Their self-fulfilling theology brings about the very abandonment they assumed had already occurred.

hist.context: Ezekiel falls facedown and intercedes — but God refuses. The blood guilt (damim) filling the land and the perversion of justice in the city have made intercession futile. This is the prophetic counterpart to Jeremiah 7:16 and 14:11, where God told Jeremiah not to pray for the people. The age of intercession has passed; the age of judgment has arrived.

rows.label: vv.3-6
rows.text: The Tav Mark and the Slaughter

lit.note: Judgment by divine council: marking the faithful, slaughtering the guilty, and the failure of prophetic intercession.

themes.note: Judgment and Justice both peak: the divine executioners carry out justice against the idolaters. Sovereignty at 9: God directs the entire operation through his angelic agents.

hebtext.note: The man in linen marks the foreheads of the faithful with a tav — the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet. In the ancient paleo-Hebrew script, tav was written as a cross or X-mark. Church fathers saw this as a foreshadowing of the cross of Christ marking believers for salvation amid judgment.

The man clothed in linen (baddim) wears priestly garments — linen is the fabric of holiness (Lev 16:4). This figure acts as both priest and scribe, marking the righteous for protection. He contrasts with the six executioners who follow him.

Ezekiel cries out: 'Will you destroy the entire remnant (she'erit) of Israel?' This is one of only two times Ezekiel intercedes (cf. 11:13). Unlike Abraham bargaining for Sodom or Moses interceding for Israel, Ezekiel's intercession receives no concession — God's response is absolute. The sin of Judah and Israel is 'exceedingly great.'

ppl.name: Ezekiel
ppl.text: Ezekiel falls facedown and intercedes — but God refuses…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The Slaughter of the Idolaters

translations.text: As I listened, he said to the others, “Follow him through the city and kill, without showing pity or compassion.
And to the others he said in my hearing, “Pass through the city after him, and strike. Your eye shall not spare, and you shall show no pity.

Now the glory of the God of Israel went up from above the cherubim, where it had been, and moved to the threshold of the temple. Then the Lord called to the man clothed in linen who had the writing kit at his side
Now the glory of the God of Israel had gone up from the cherub on which it rested to the threshold of the house. And he called to the man clothed in linen, who had the writing case at his waist.

rec.title: John MacArthur
rec.quote: "Six men with destroying weapons come from the direction of the upper gate — the north, the direction of Babylon. The agents of judgment approach from the direction of the coming army."

thread.text: The Passover blood on doorframes protected Israelites from the destroying angel — the tav mark echoes this protective sign.
Angels seal the servants of God on their foreheads before judgment falls — directly echoing Ezekiel's vision.
Judgment begins at the house of God — Peter's principle matches Ezekiel 9:6, where the slaughter starts at the sanctuary.
Abraham interceded for Sodom and found less than ten righteous — Ezekiel faces a similar moment but receives a harsher answer.
God told Jeremiah: 'Do not pray for this people' — both prophets learn that intercession has limits when judgment is decreed.

coaching.tip: A man in linen marks the foreheads of those who 'grieve and lament' over Jerusalem's abominations (v. 4). The mark (tav, the last letter of the Hebrew alphabet, shaped like a cross in ancient script) protects the faithful. Revelation 7:3 echoes this: the servants of God are sealed on their foreheads before judgment falls. The remnant is identified not by performance but by grief over sin.

Ezekiel cries out: 'Are you going to destroy the entire remnant of Israel?' (v. 8). The prophet who delivers judgment also protests it — like Abraham bargaining for Sodom. Prophetic faithfulness includes arguing with God on behalf of the condemned. The tension between duty (deliver the message) and compassion (protest the outcome) is never resolved in Ezekiel. It simply coexists.
